{{short description|Microorganisms living in the fruit fly}}
{{DISPLAYTITLE:Microbiome in the ''Drosophila'' gut}}
{{microbiomes|other}}

The [[microbiota]] are the sum of all symbiotic [[microorganisms]] ([[mutualism (biology)|mutualistic]], [[commensalism|commensal]] or [[pathogenic]]) living on or in an organism. The fruit fly ''[[Drosophila melanogaster]]'' is a [[model organism]] and known as one of the most investigated organisms worldwide. The microbiota in flies is less complex than that found in humans. It still has an influence on the fitness of the fly,<ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=Gould AL, Zhang V, Lamberti L, Jones EW, Obadia B, Korasidis N, Gavryushkin A, Carlson JM, Beerenwinkel N, Ludington WB |display-authors=6 |title=Microbiome interactions shape host fitness |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America |volume=115 |issue=51 |pages=E11951–E11960 |date=December 2018 |pmid=30510004 |pmc=6304949 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1809349115 |bibcode=2018PNAS..11511951G |doi-access=free}}</ref> and it affects different life-history characteristics such as lifespan ([[life expectancy]]), resistance against pathogens ([[immunity (medical)|immunity]]) and metabolic processes ([[digestion]]). Considering the comprehensive toolkit available for research in ''Drosophila'', analysis of its microbiome could enhance our understanding of similar processes in other types of host-microbiota interactions, including those involving humans. Microbiota plays key roles in the intestinal immune and metabolic responses via their fermentation product ([[Short-chain fatty acid|short chain fatty acid]]), [[acetate]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Jugder |first1=Bat-Erdene |last2=Kamareddine |first2=Layla |last3=Watnick |first3=Paula I. |date=2021 |title=Microbiota-derived acetate activates intestinal innate immunity via the Tip60 histone acetyltransferase complex |url=|journal=Immunity |volume=54 |issue=8 |pages=1683–1697.e3 |doi=10.1016/j.immuni.2021.05.017 |pmid=34107298 |pmc=8363570 |issn=1074-7613}}</ref>

==Microbial composition==
''Drosophila melanogaster'' possesses a comparatively simple gut microbiota, consisting of only few bacterial species, mainly from two bacterial taxonomic groups: [[Bacillota]] and [[Pseudomonadota]].<ref name="Erkosar 2013" >{{cite journal |vauthors=Erkosar B, Storelli G, Defaye A, Leulier F |title=Host-intestinal microbiota mutualism: "learning on the fly" |journal=Cell Host & Microbe |volume=13 |issue=1 |pages=8–14 |date=January 2013 |pmid=23332152 |doi=10.1016/j.chom.2012.12.004 |doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name="Staubach 2013" >{{cite journal |vauthors=Staubach F, Baines JF, Künzel S, Bik EM, Petrov DA |title=Host species and environmental effects on bacterial communities associated with ''Drosophila'' in the laboratory and in the natural environment |journal=PLOS ONE |volume=8 |issue=8 |article-number=e70749 |year=2013 |pmid=23967097 |pmc=3742674 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0070749 |arxiv=1211.3367 |bibcode=2013PLoSO...870749S |doi-access=free}}</ref> The most common species belong to the families [[Lactobacillaceae]] (abundance of approx. 30%, members of the Bacillota) and [[Acetobacteraceae]] (approx. 55%, members of the Proteobacteria). Other less common bacterial species are from the families Leuconostocaceae, Enterococaceae, and Enterobacteriaceae (all with an abundance in between 2–4%).<ref name="Staubach 2013" /> The most common species include ''[[Lactobacillus plantarum]]'', ''[[Lactobacillus brevis]]'', ''[[Acetobacter pomorum]]'' and ''[[Enterococcus faecalis]]'', while other species such as ''[[Acetobacter aceti]]'', ''[[Acetobacter tropicalis]]'' and ''[[Acetobacter pasteurianus]]'' are also often found.<ref name="Erkosar 2013" />

The particular species of the host fly has a central influence on the composition and quality of the gut microbiota, even if flies are raised under similar conditions.<ref name="Boderick 2012" >{{cite journal |vauthors=Broderick NA, Lemaitre B |title=Gut-associated microbes of ''Drosophila melanogaster'' |journal=Gut Microbes |volume=3 |issue=4 |pages=307–21 |year=2012 |pmid=22572876 |pmc=3463489 |doi=10.4161/gmic.19896}}</ref> Nevertheless, the host's diet and nutritional environment also shape the exact composition of the microbiota. For instance the exact [[pH]] of the food can kill certain bacterial species.<ref name="Erkosar 2013" /> In general, the type of food used by the fly affects the microbiota composition.<ref name="Chandler 2011" >{{cite journal |vauthors=Chandler JA, Lang JM, Bhatnagar S, Eisen JA, Kopp A |title=Bacterial communities of diverse ''Drosophila'' species: ecological context of a host-microbe model system |journal=PLOS Genetics |volume=7 |issue=9 |article-number=e1002272 |date=September 2011 |pmid=21966276 |pmc=3178584 |doi=10.1371/journal.pgen.1002272 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Mushroom feeder species like ''[[Drosophila falleni]]'' and ''[[Microdrosophila]]'' harbour many Lactobacillales and generally maintain high bacterial diversity in their guts. The microbiota of flower feeders such as ''[[Drosophila elegans]]'' and ''[[Drosophila flavohirta]]'' shows higher abundance of Enterobacteriaceae and to a lesser extent of acido-philic bacteria (such as Acetobacteraceae and Lactobacillaceae) if compared to fruit-eating species such as ''[[Drosophila hydei]]'', ''[[Drosophila immigrans]]'', ''[[Drosophila sulfurigaster]]'', ''Drosophila melanogaster'', ''[[Drosophila sechellia]]'' or ''[[Drosophila takahashii]]''.<ref name="Erkosar 2013" /> The microbial load and bacterial composition also vary with the age of the host fly.<ref name="Erkosar 2013" />

==Microbiota transmission and establishment==
Feeding is a key determinant of the microbiota composition. Not only the diet influences presence and abundance of the bacteria inside the gut, but the bacteria also need to be taken up continuously from the environment to prevail as members of the intestinal flora.<ref name="Storelli 2018" >{{cite journal |vauthors=Storelli G, Strigini M, Grenier T, Bozonnet L, Schwarzer M, Daniel C, Matos R, Leulier F |title=''Drosophila'' Perpetuates Nutritional Mutualism by Promoting the Fitness of Its Intestinal Symbiont Lactobacillus plantarum |journal=Cell Metab. |volume=27 |issue=2 |pages=362–377 |date=February 2018 |pmid=29290388 |pmc=5807057 |doi=10.1016/j.cmet.2017.11.011}}</ref> Feeding on feces seems to play a central role for establishment of the ''Drosophila'' microbiota, as it allows the flies to recycle the bacteria within a fly population at a particular time point and also across generations. Flies seed the embryonic eggshell with feces. Upon hatching, young larvae eat their eggshells and thereby pick up the bacteria. The microbiota, which subsequently establishes itself inside the gut of the developing larvae, is similar to that of the larvae's mothers.<ref name="Blum 2013" >{{cite journal |vauthors=Blum JE, Fischer CN, Miles J, Handelsman J |title=Frequent replenishment sustains the beneficial microbiome of ''Drosophila melanogaster'' |journal=mBio |volume=4 |issue=6 |pages=e00860-13 |date=November 2013 |pmid=24194543 |pmc=3892787 |doi=10.1128/mbio.00860-13 |doi-access=free}}</ref>
This may further be promoted by the particular life history of the flies. Young adult flies, which harbor fewer bacteria than old flies, proliferate in an environment shaped by the feces of the preceding fly generation, thus allowing them to take up additional bacteria.<ref name="Blum 2013" />

==Gut compartmentalization==
[[File:Drosophila melanogaster digestive tract.png|thumb|upright=1.5| {{center|'''''Drosophila melanogaster'' gut'''}} The gut is anatomically and functionally divided into three major regions: the [[foregut]], [[midgut]], and [[hindgut]]. The foregut includes the esophagus and crop, functioning in food storage and initial mechanical digestion. The midgut is the primary site for enzymatic digestion and nutrient absorption. The hindgut is involved in water reabsorption and waste excretion. R0–R4 indicate the further division of the midgut into five smaller regions.]]

[[File:Comparison between human and Drosophila gut.png|thumb|upright=1.5| {{center|'''Comparison between human and ''Drosophila'' gut'''}} Organs with similar functions are coded with same colors. ''Drosophila'' contains many tissues/organs that functionally resemble to most essential human gastrointestinal system: [[Esophagus]] ([[foregut]]), [[midgut]] ([[small intestine]]) and [[large intestine]] ([[hindgut]]), [[stomach]] ([[crop]]), [[kidney]]s ([[malpighian tubule]]s).]]

In the [[gut (anatomy)|gut]] (digestive tract) of ''Drosophila melanogaster'' the composition and action of the microbiome appears to be tightly regulated within compartments, that is different sections of the intestines. This is indicated by the differential expression of genes, especially with a regulatory function, in the [[epithelium]] of different parts of the gut. In detail, the gut is compartmentalized into three parts, the ''foregut'', the ''midgut'', and the ''hindgut''. While foregut and hindgut are lined with a cuticle formed by the [[ectodermal]] epithelium, the midgut is of [[endodermal]] origin.<ref name="Lemaitre 2013" >{{cite journal |vauthors=Lemaitre B, Miguel-Aliaga I |title=The digestive tract of ''Drosophila melanogaster'' |journal=Annual Review of Genetics |volume=47 |pages=377–404 |year=2013 |pmid=24016187 |doi=10.1146/annurev-genet-111212-133343 |url=http://infoscience.epfl.ch/record/191313}}</ref> In adult flies the midgut is further divided into five smaller regions.<ref name="Buchon 2013" >{{cite journal |vauthors=Buchon N, Osman D, David FP, Fang HY, Boquete JP, Deplancke B, Lemaitre B |title=Morphological and molecular characterization of adult midgut compartmentalization in ''Drosophila'' |journal=Cell Reports |volume=3 |issue=5 |pages=1725–38 |date=May 2013 |pmid=23643535 |doi=10.1016/j.celrep.2013.04.001 |doi-access=free}}</ref> The immune response varies among the gut regions. The [[Immunodeficiency|immune deficiency]] (IMD) pathway responds to bacterial infections and is activated by certain receptors (e.g., the peptidoglycan receptor protein [[PGRP-LC]]). These receptors and also other components of the ''Drosophila'' immune system such as Toll receptor and [[dDUOX]] pathway molecules control immune responses in ectodermal tissue of the anterior gut. Moreover, the anterior midgut is enriched in certain antimicrobial peptides ([[AMPs]]). This suggests that the immune defence in this area is particularly responsive, possibly because this regions represents the first contact region for newly taken up food, microbiota, and/or intestinal pathogens. In the middle and posterior midgut, other genes such as the receptor [[PGRP-LB]], which down-regulates the IMD immune response, are expressed, possibly in order to minimize expression of immune defence against the microbiota. In addition, the microbiota itself seems to control the expression of several ''Drosophila'' metabolic genes within the midgut, possibly to facilitate digestion of food.<ref name="Broderick 2014" >{{cite journal |vauthors=Broderick NA, Buchon N, Lemaitre B |title=Microbiota-induced changes in ''Drosophila melanogaster'' host gene expression and gut morphology |journal=mBio |volume=5 |issue=3 |pages=e01117-14 |date=May 2014 |pmid=24865556 |pmc=4045073 |doi=10.1128/mbio.01117-14 |doi-access=free}}</ref> Recently, IMD pathway in the anterior midgut region has been proposed to play multi-pronged roles to modulate key metabolic and mechanic functions in the gut.<ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=Watnick PI, Jugder BE |title=Microbial Control of Intestinal Homeostasis via Enteroendocrine Cell Innate Immune Signaling |journal=Trends in Microbiology |volume=28 |issue=2 |pages=141–149 |date=February 2020 |pmid=31699645 |pmc=6980660 |doi=10.1016/j.tim.2019.09.005}}</ref> Taken together, it appears that the interaction between host and microbiota is precisely regulated across different regions within the gut.<ref name="Bosco-Drayon 2012" >{{cite journal |vauthors=Bosco-Drayon V, Poidevin M, Boneca IG, Narbonne-Reveau K, Royet J, Charroux B |title=Peptidoglycan sensing by the receptor PGRP-LE in the ''Drosophila'' gut induces immune responses to infectious bacteria and tolerance to microbiota |journal=Cell Host & Microbe |volume=12 |issue=2 |pages=153–65 |date=August 2012 |pmid=22901536 |doi=10.1016/j.chom.2012.06.002 |doi-access=free}}</ref>

==Effects on behaviour==
''Drosophila'' microbiota have been implicated in mating success by influencing assortative mating; a phenomenon detected in some studies of ''Drosophila'',<ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=Sharon G, Segal D, Ringo JM, Hefetz A, Zilber-Rosenberg I, Rosenberg E |title=Commensal bacteria play a role in mating preference of ''Drosophila melanogaster'' |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America |volume=107 |issue=46 |pages=20051–6 |date=November 2010 |pmid=21041648 |pmc=2993361 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1009906107 |doi-access=free}}</ref> but not others.<ref name="Leftwich-et-al-bundle">
{{Unbulleted list citebundle
|{{cite journal |vauthors=Leftwich PT, Clarke NV, Hutchings MI, Chapman T |title=''Drosophila'' |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America |volume=114 |issue=48 |pages=12767–12772 |date=November 2017 |pmid=29109277 |pmc=5715749 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1708345114 |doi-access=free}}
 |{{cite journal |last1=Miguel-Aliaga |first1=Irene |last2=Jasper |first2=Heinrich |last3=Lemaitre |first3=Bruno |title=Anatomy and Physiology of the Digestive Tract of ''Drosophila melanogaster'' |journal=[[Genetics (journal)|Genetics]] |publisher=[[Genetics Society of America]] ([[Oxford University Press|OUP]]) |volume=210 |issue=2 |date=2018-10-01 |issn=1943-2631 |doi=10.1534/genetics.118.300224 |pages=357–396 |s2cid=52922044 |pmid=30287514 |pmc=6216580}}
 |{{cite journal |last=Douglas |first=Angela E. |title=Simple animal models for microbiome research |journal=Nature Reviews Microbiology |volume=17 |issue=12 |date=2019-08-15 |issn=1740-1534 |doi=10.1038/s41579-019-0242-1 |pages=764–775 |s2cid=199661914 |pmid=31417197}} {{small|1=(AED [[ORCID]]: [http://orcid.org/0000-0001-5212-6826 0000-0001-5212-6826])}}.
|{{cite journal |vauthors=Leftwich PT, Hutchings MI, Chapman T |title=Diet, Gut Microbes and Host Mate Choice: Understanding the significance of microbiome effects on host mate choice requires a case by case evaluation |journal=BioEssays |volume=40 |issue=12 |article-number=e1800053 |date=December 2018 |pmid=30311675 |doi=10.1002/bies.201800053 |doi-access=free}}
 |{{cite journal |last1=Cusick |first1=Jessica A. |last2=Wellman |first2=Cara L. |last3=Demas |first3=Gregory E. |title=The call of the wild: using non-model systems to investigate microbiome–behaviour relationships |journal=Journal of Experimental Biology |publisher=The Company of Biologists |volume=224 |issue=10 |date=2021-05-14 |issn=0022-0949 |doi=10.1242/jeb.224485 |pmid=33988717 |pmc=8180253 |bibcode=2021JExpB.224B4485C |s2cid=234497490}}
}}</ref>

==Effects on longevity==
The microbiota seem to affect the lifespan of ''Drosophila melanogaster''. To date, the mechanisms of this effect remain elusive.<br />
Fruit flies raised under [[axenic]] conditions (i.e., without any bacteria in the environment) or cured of their microbiota with [[antibiotics]] had a shorter lifespan than flies raised under normal conditions. The microbiota influence on longevity seems to be particularly strong early in development.<ref name="Brummerl 2004" >{{cite journal |vauthors=Brummel T, Ching A, Seroude L, Simon AF, Benzer S |title=''Drosophila'' lifespan enhancement by exogenous bacteria |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America |volume=101 |issue=35 |pages=12974–9 |date=August 2004 |pmid=15322271 |pmc=516503 |doi=10.1073/pnas.0405207101 |bibcode=2004PNAS..10112974B |doi-access=free}}</ref>
To date, however, the exact mechanisms underlying these effects remain elusive. It is possible that the microbiota-induced proliferation of intestinal [[stem cells]] and associated metabolic [[homeostasis]] is important in this context.<ref name="Shin 2011" >{{cite journal |vauthors=Shin SC, Kim SH, You H, Kim B, Kim AC, Lee KA, Yoon JH, Ryu JH, Lee WJ |s2cid=206536986 |display-authors=6 |title=''Drosophila'' microbiome modulates host developmental and metabolic homeostasis via insulin signaling |journal=Science |volume=334 |issue=6056 |pages=670–4 |date=November 2011 |pmid=22053049 |doi=10.1126/science.1212782 |bibcode=2011Sci...334..670S}}</ref> In contrast, the microbiota seems to have a negative effect on lifespan in older ''Drosophila melanogaster'', because their removal in ageing flies increases longevity. Old flies have a reduced ability to fight infections and this may also relate to the bacterial members of the microbiota.<ref name="Ramsden 2008" >{{cite journal |vauthors=Ramsden S, Cheung YY, Seroude L |title=Functional analysis of the ''Drosophila'' immune response during aging |journal=Aging Cell |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=225–36 |date=March 2008 |pmid=18221416 |doi=10.1111/j.1474-9726.2008.00370.x |s2cid=8510421}}</ref> In aged animals, immune responses may over-shoot, possibly harming the host and favoring colonization with pathogens (e.g. ''[[Gluconobacter morbifer]]'').<ref name="Ryu 2008" >{{cite journal |vauthors=Ryu JH, Kim SH, Lee HY, Bai JY, Nam YD, Bae JW, Lee DG, Shin SC, Ha EM, Lee WJ |s2cid=23798836 |display-authors=6 |title=Innate immune homeostasis by the homeobox gene caudal and commensal-gut mutualism in ''Drosophila'' |journal=Science |volume=319 |issue=5864 |pages=777–82 |date=February 2008 |pmid=18218863 |doi=10.1126/science.1149357 |bibcode=2008Sci...319..777R|doi-access=free }}</ref>

==New methods of microbiome analysis==

Almost all current approaches for the characterization of ''Drosophila'' microbiota rely on destructive approaches, that is flies are killed, their gut is extracted and from these the bacteria are isolated and/or analyzed. For an assessment of microbiota dynamics across the lifespan of an individual fly or across development of a fly population, a non-destructive approach would be favorable. Such an approach was recently developed, focusing on the microbial characterization of fly feces. Fly feces are indeed informative on composition of the gut microbiota, since the diversity of gut bacteria, feces bacteria and bacteria of whole fly of ''Drosophila melanogaster'' are all strongly correlated. This new approach could be used to demonstrate the known influence of diets.<ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=Fink C, Staubach F, Kuenzel S, Baines JF, Roeder T |title=Noninvasive analysis of microbiome dynamics in the fruit fly ''Drosophila melanogaster'' |journal=Applied and Environmental Microbiology |volume=79 |issue=22 |pages=6984–8 |date=November 2013 |pmid=24014528 |pmc=3811555 |doi=10.1128/AEM.01903-13 |bibcode=2013ApEnM..79.6984F}}</ref>
